Morgan"},"description":"Hi! The Ripped Body® Method is an evidence-based, data-driven approach to physique transformation. ⠀ For the majority of my 20s, I fell for the scams of the fitness industry. 🤦🏻‍♂️ ⠀ This site is my revenge. 😬 ⠀ Since 2011, I’ve written guides explaining how we get results to help readers avoid the same path of frustration. We’ve helped over 2,000 busy working guys transform their bodies WITHOUT supplements, fad diets, or the process dominating their lives. ⠀ 📩 Enter your email address in the box at the bottom of this page and I'd be honored to show you how.","sameAs":["https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/","https:\/\/www.facebook.com\/rippedbodycom","https:\/\/www.instagram.com\/andy_rippedbody\/","https:\/\/andymorgan.com\/","https:\/\/www.amazon.com\/Andy-John-Morgan\/e\/B07Q2J5ST6\/ref=ntt_dp_epwbk_2","https:\/\/x.com\/https:\/\/twitter.com\/RippedBodyjp","https:\/\/muscleandstrengthpyramids.com\/","https:\/\/soundcloud.com\/andyrippedbodyjp","morganandrew"]},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/maximum-muscular-potential\/#faq-question-1595913829199","position":1,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/maximum-muscular-potential\/#faq-question-1595913829199","name":"How do you calculate maximum muscular potential?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Maximum muscular potential isn't calculable. There are models that <em>claim<\/em> to be able to predict it, which I introduce in this article, but they have severe limitations. It’s essential to keep these models and their limitations in mind when you <a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/how-to-bulk\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noreferrer noopener\">plan your bulk<\/a>. This helps to ground us in reality rather than fantasy land, ensuring that we don’t fall into the ‘<a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/dreambulkeritis\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noreferrer noopener\">dream bulk’ trap<\/a>, gain a ton of fat, and waste a lot of time and effort dieting it off again.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/maximum-muscular-potential\/#faq-question-1595914886263","position":2,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/maximum-muscular-potential\/#faq-question-1595914886263","name":"Is there a genetic limit to muscle growth.","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Yes. As you advance as a trainee, you can expect diminishing returns to your training efforts. You'll make the best gains during the first year of serious training, as long as you get your nutrition strategy right also. See my free guide: <a href=\"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/complete-diet-nutrition-set-up-guide\/\">The RippedBody Nutrition Setup Guide<\/a>.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/maximum-muscular-potential\/#faq-question-1595915030751","position":3,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/maximum-muscular-potential\/#faq-question-1595915030751","name":"What is FFMI?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"FFMI (Fat-Free Mass Index) measures the amount of muscle mass someone has relative to their height. In other words, it's a way to quantify how jacked people are.<br\/><br\/>It is calculated as follows: <em>FFMI = fat-free mass in kg ÷ (height in meters)²<\/em><br\/><br\/>You can use FFMI to estimate how close you are to your genetic potential, which can help inform your training decisions.","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"},{"@type":"Question","@id":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/maximum-muscular-potential\/#faq-question-1595917042925","position":4,"url":"https:\/\/rippedbody.com\/maximum-muscular-potential\/#faq-question-1595917042925","name":"Why do muscles stop growing?","answerCount":1,"acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"It's not known for sure, but it may be a surface area to muscle volume ratio problem related to energy and oxygen delivery.<br\/><br\/>Once muscle fibers get too big, it may be too energetically costly to build more protein and maintain the fiber as it is, and there may be something telling the fiber that it's no longer economical to build. <br\/><br\/>For example, when muscle fibers get too large, they can start having a problem with inorganic phosphate accumulation which can affect contractile function. Additionally, mitochondrial density tends to drop, capillary density per fiber tends to be maintained, but per unit of cross-sectional area tends to decrease. (Source: <a href=\"https:\/\/the-stronger-by-science-podcast.simplecast.com\/episodes\/reading-research-the-placebo-diet-muscle-memory-and-alex-kolliari-turner?t=2h29m52s\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noreferrer noopener\">Stronger by Science Episode<\/a> <a href=\"https:\/\/the-stronger-by-science-podcast.simplecast.com\/episodes\/reading-research-the-placebo-diet-muscle-memory-and-alex-kolliari-turner?t=2h29m52s\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noreferrer noopener\">20: Reading Research, the Placebo Diet, Muscle Memory, and Alex Kolliari-Turner<\/a>)","inLanguage":"en-US"},"inLanguage":"en-US"}]}</script> <link href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin="anonymous" rel="preconnect"><link rel="alternate" title="oEmbed (JSON)" type="application/json+oembed" href="https://rippedbody.com/wp-json/oembed/1.0/embed?url=https%3A%2F%2Frippedbody.com%2Fmaximum-muscular-potential%2F"><link rel="alternate" title="oEmbed (XML)" type="text/xml+oembed" href="https://rippedbody.com/wp-json/oembed/1.0/embed?url=https%3A%2F%2Frippedbody.com%2Fmaximum-muscular-potential%2F&format=xml"><style id="wp-img-auto-sizes-contain-inline-css">img:is([sizes=auto i],[sizes^="auto," i]){contain-intrinsic-size:3000px 1500px}

  // Helper function to read a cookie value
  function getCookie(name) {
    var value = "; " + document.cookie;
    var parts = value.split("; " + name + "=");
    if (parts.length == 2) return parts.pop().split(";").shift();
  }

  // Helper function to set a cookie
  function setCookie(name, value, days) {
    var expires = "";
    if (days) {
      var date = new Date();
      date.setTime(date.getTime() + (days*24*60*60*1000));
      expires = "; expires=" + date.toUTCString();
    }
    document.cookie = name + "=" + (value || "")  + expires + "; path=/";
  }

  // Function to update display eligibility based on the time gap
  function updateDisplayEligibility(waitTimeSeconds) {
    setTimeout(function() {
      window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'true';
      console.log('updateDisplayEligibility - OptinMonster is now display eligible');
    }, waitTimeSeconds * 1000); // Convert seconds to milliseconds
  }

  document.addEventListener('om.Campaign.show', function(event) {
    // console.log('om.Campaign.show', event.detail.Campaign);

    var now = new Date().getTime();
    setCookie('optinMonsterLastDisplayTimestamp', now, 1); // Store timestamp in cookie for 1 day
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'false';

    updateDisplayEligibility(minimumDisplayGap);
  });

  var now = new Date().getTime();
  var lastDisplayTime = parseInt(getCookie('optinMonsterLastDisplayTimestamp')) || 0;
  var timePassed = (now - lastDisplayTime) / 1000; // Convert milliseconds to seconds

  if (timePassed >= minimumDisplayGap) {
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'true';
    console.log('OptinMonster display eligible on page load');
  } else {
    var waitTimeSeconds = minimumDisplayGap - timePassed;
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'false';
    updateDisplayEligibility(waitTimeSeconds);
  }</script> <style id="uagb-style-conditional-extension">@media (min-width: 1025px){body .uag-hide-desktop.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-desktop{display:none !important}}@media (min-width: 768px) and (max-width: 1024px){body .uag-hide-tab.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-tab{display:none !important}}@media (max-width: 767px){body .uag-hide-mob.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-mob{display:none !important}}</style><style id="uagb-style-frontend-62682">.uagb-heading-text mark {

#555555 !important;}.formkit-form[data-uid="8462769b72"] .formkit-submit{border:1px solid #333333 !important;}</style></form><div id="modal-ready"><p><strong>Key points:</strong></p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>There are limitations to the maximum drug-free muscular potential models out there.</li><li>It’s useful to be aware of them so that you don’t set yourself silly expectations and just get fat when bulking, but don’t allow them to place limiting beliefs on what you are capable of as you get close to these numbers.</li><li>As fun as it would be, it is not possible to simply make a calculation to determine whether someone has taken steroids.</li></ul><p>I am guilty of linking far too many people to a couple of articles on genetic muscular potential over the years, believing the formulas and calculations discussed within to be <em>the</em> definitive word on the subject of what a drug-free athlete can achieve.</p><p>They are not. This article is here to correct that.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">MAXIMUM DRUG-FREE GENETIC MUSCULAR POTENTIAL MODELS</h2><p>The models in question:</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>Martin Berkhan’s <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="https://leangains.com/maximum-muscular-potential-of-drug-free-athletes-updated-dec-31st/" target="_blank"><em>Maximum Muscular Potential of Drug-Free Athletes</em></a><em><strong> </strong></em>calculation.</li><li>Casey Butt’s Your <em>Muscular Potential</em>.</li></ul><h3 class="wp-block-heading">THE BERKHAN MODEL OF MAXIMUM DRUG-FREE GENETIC POTENTIAL</h3><p>Though I’d highly recommend that you read the article, Martin’s formula for determining the maximum that a drug-free trainee will weigh when in “<em>stage-shredded”</em> condition (i.e. 5-6% body fat) is as follows:</p><p><em><strong>(Height in cm – 98~102) = Bodyweight in kg.</strong></em></p><p>This works out as follows:</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li><strong>173cm</strong> <strong>tall</strong> <strong>(5’8)</strong>, your stage-shredded maximum will be <strong>~75kg (165lbs)</strong>.</li><li><strong>178cm tall (5’10)</strong> your stage-shredded maximum will be <strong>~</strong><strong>80kg (176lbs)</strong>.</li><li><strong>183cm tall (6’0)</strong> your stage-shredded maximum will be <strong>~</strong><strong>85kg (187lbs)</strong>.</li><li><strong>188cm tall (6’2)</strong> your stage-shredded maximum will be <strong>~</strong><strong>90kg (198lbs)</strong>.</li></ul><p>While this is not a bad rule of thumb for estimating what a seriously hard-working trainee will achieve over a lifetime, it doesn’t take into account the genetically blessed exceptions to the rule. We’ll look at these exceptions now.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity"><h3 class="wp-block-heading">USING FFMI TO DETERMINE WHAT CAN BE ACHIEVED AS A NATURAL BODYBUILDER</h3><p>Eric Helms wrote an excellent guest article for Alan Aragon’s Research Review recently, <em><a href="http://www.alanaragonblog.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/11/Aug-2014-AARR-Eric-Helms-Article.pdf">What can be achieved as a natural bodybuilder?</a> </em>Alan decided that it was such an important piece that he published it for free, and I’d encourage you to read it. (For those that won’t be bothered, from here to the table graphic below is a quick summary.)</p><p>Eric writes about some famous research from 1995, that looked at the fat-free mass index (FFMI) of steroid using and non-steroid using bodybuilders.</p><p><strong>FFMI = fat-free mass in kg ÷ (height in meters)²</strong></p><p>In the research 83 users, 74 nonusers FFMI were calculated and then compared. The average FFMI of the steroid users was ~25, of non-users it was ~22, and on the extreme ends a handful of non-users reached a FFMI of ~25.</p><p>To put that into more meaningful figures, the average height of the men in both groups was ~180cm (5’11), average body-fat percentage ~13%. The average weight of the steroid users was 92kg (202.5lbs), vs the non-users which was 82kg (180.5lbs).</p><p>The maximum FFMI of the non-user group was 25; for the steroid-using group it was 32.</p><p><strong>From this research, it was suggested that the genetic ceiling for a natural trainee was a FFMI of around 25.</strong></p><p><strong>Comparison With The <em>Berkhan</em> Model</strong></p><p>Here’s how the Berkhan model looks when the predicted figures from his model are converted to FFMI:</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>173cm (5’8), 75kg (165lbs) @5% = 71.25kg lean mass = <strong>23.8 FFMI</strong></li><li>178cm (5’10), 80kg (176lbs) @5% = 76kg lean mass = <strong>24.0 FFMI</strong></li><li>183cm (6’0), 85kg (187lbs) @5% = 80.75kg lean mass = <strong>24.1 FFMI</strong></li><li>188cm (6’2), ~90kg (198lbs) @5% = 85.5kg lean mass = <strong>24.2 FFMI</strong></li></ul><p>We can see that the Berkhan model figures are lower, and the math works out to be ~6.5-8lbs (~3-3.5kg) lower than an FFMI of 25.</p><p>[optin-monster-inline slug=”dbpjihoivuka5yblnu35″]</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity"><h3 class="wp-block-heading">RESEARCH VS HISTORY</h3><p>Eric then goes on to talk about the FFMI data of the Mr America winners from 1939-1959, spanning a period where steroids were almost certainly not used, to a time when they were, that was also discussed in that study.</p><p><em>Using Table 2 from Kouri et al, high scepticism is indicated by the winners in the green box, while moderate scepticism is indicated by the winners in the orange box. If we accept the 1939-1944 winners as natural, the average FFMI is 24.9, with the highest reported at 27.3. Applying moderate skepticism and accepting the 1939-1953 winners as natural, the average FFMI is 25.6, with the highest reported at 28.0. These means are not much different from the 1939-1959 group mean. In fact, the authors analysed the FFMI’s to determine if they were increasing over time. They noted: “there was no significant trend towards increased FFMI among the Mr. America winners over a 20-year span from 1939 to 1959.” Thus if drug use was occurring, perhaps it wasn’t frequent or effective enough to significantly affect the aggregate FFMI.</em></p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter"><img decoding="async" width="595" height="621" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/06/Mr-America-Winner-FFMIs-1939-1959.jpg" alt="Mr America Winner FFMIs - 1939-1959" class="wp-image-39844" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/06/Mr-America-Winner-FFMIs-1939-1959.jpg 595w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/06/Mr-America-Winner-FFMIs-1939-1959-431x450.jpg 431w" sizes="(max-width: 595px) 100vw, 595px"></figure><p><strong>One thing worth mentioning, </strong>as it’s an issue I’m sure people will raise: ‘golden age’ bodybuilders usually carried more fat on stage than guys today (8-10% was more common than the 5-6% we expect now) which means their FFMIs were overstated a bit. On the flip side, awesome leg development wasn’t expected or desirable (compare Arnold’s legs to literally any pro BB today), which probably more than makes up for the difference. (i.e. if those guys were competing today, they’d probably have 3-4kg (~7-9lb) less fat, but at least 3-4kg more muscle on their legs and ass.)</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity"><h3 class="wp-block-heading">CRITICISMS OF THE 25 FFMI GENETIC CEILING</h3><p><strong>1) There were guys with a FFMI over 25 that were almost certainly not on steroids.</strong> You can see from the table, one guy had a FFMI of 27.3, and another with a FFMI of 26.9, and if we lower our scepticism just a bit, we can take it all the way up to 28.</p><p><strong>2) If we’re going to take a FFMI of 25 (or even 27.3 or 28) as the absolute limit of what’s attainable naturally, we would also have to assume that the most genetically elite people imaginable are included in that sample, which is not likely</strong>. Yes, those guys had physiques that were on par with the top drug-tested bodybuilders today (and some lifters of that era, including 26.9 FFMI Grimek, put up weights that are still very impressive by today’s standards), but lifting and physique sports have grown considerably since the ’40s and ’50s.</p><p>There is certainly the possibility that there are some people better suited for gaining muscle mass currently participating in bodybuilding and strength sports now, unless we can be sure that the bodybuilders from the ’30s and ’40s represent the absolute pinnacle of attainable human muscularity. However, the records in every other sport have progressed over time as better genetic specimens have entered the pool of competitors (I’d suggest <em>The Sports Gene</em> by David Epstein for more information), so I’m not sure why we’d assume levels of muscularity in drug-free bodybuilding would be any different.</p><p><strong>3) We also have to assume that we’ve made no progress whatsoever in terms of training, nutrition, and supplementation.</strong> The cynic in me believes that we really haven’t made that much progress in terms of training for hypertrophy since a) periodization doesn’t seem to play much of a role, and b) the biggest determining factor for muscle growth just seems to be <a href="https://www.strongerbyscience.com/the-new-approach-to-training-volume/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">training hard and doing a bunch of hard sets</a>, with other factors playing a much smaller role. Basically, getting big isn’t rocket science – it’s mostly about hard work.</p><p>However, I do think we’ve made some progress in terms of nutrition and <a href="https://rippedbody.com/supplements/" data-wpil-monitor-id="279">supplementation</a>. I mean, take something as basic as creatine – those guys didn’t have access to it unless they were eating a lot of raw red meat. Will it make a night-and-day difference? No. But it has been shown to pretty reliably help people gain a little more muscle mass.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity"><h3 class="wp-block-heading">Concluding Points</h3><p>It’s essential to keep these models and their limitations in mind when you <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-bulk/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">plan your bulk</a>. This helps to ground us in reality rather than fantasy land, ensuring that we don’t fall into the ‘<a href="https://rippedbody.com/dreambulkeritis/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">dream bulk’ trap</a>, gain a ton of fat, and waste a lot of time and effort dieting it off again.</p><p>The <em>Casey Butt</em> / <em>Berkhan</em> model calculations are probably best described as roughly an average of what’s attainable for most reasonably “blessed” people.</p><p>A FFMI of 25 is the number usually thrown around as the upper limit, even for someone with <em>great</em> genetics. But as we’ve seen, there are people who go beyond that.</p><p>We <em>know</em> that a FFMI of at least 27.3 is attainable naturally, and there it’s almost certain that there are people beyond this.</p><p>Add in some minor advancements in training, nutrition, and supplementation, along with some skepticism about whether the most elite of the elite genetically were competing in bodybuilding in the ’40s, and I think it’s pretty likely that some people can attain an FFMI in the 28s naturally.</p><p>Can many people? Absolutely not.</p><p>Can most people reach the more conservative limit of 25? Nope. Your genetics are your genetics, and some people were simply dealt a better hand than others.</p><p>But an FFMI over 25 definitely doesn’t automatically mean someone’s using drugs. And if you have rocketed your way up to the genetic limits calculated by the Berkhan model in your first few years of training, there’s every reason to believe you are one of the few that has the potential to go on and exceed it.</p><p>So, don’t limit yourself based on these formulas. <a href="https://www.strongerbyscience.com/strong-size-of-your-pond/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">The mind exerts a powerful effect on the body</a>. Believe in yourself and have the mindset that you can, and you may just do so.</p><p>Here’s an excellent article by my friend Greg Nuckols over on Strongerbyscience.com on how to calculate <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="https://www.strongerbyscience.com/your-drug-free-muscle-and-strength-potential-part-1/" target="_blank"><em>YOUR Drug-Free Muscle and Strength Potential</em></a>.</p><p>Thank you for reading.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h3 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">Maximum Muscular Potential FAQ</h3><div class="schema-faq wp-block-yoast-faq-block"><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1595913829199"><strong class="schema-faq-question">How do you calculate maximum muscular potential?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">Maximum muscular potential isn’t calculable. There are models that <em>claim</em> to be able to predict it, which I introduce in this article, but they have severe limitations. It’s essential to keep these models and their limitations in mind when you <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-to-bulk/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">plan your bulk</a>. This helps to ground us in reality rather than fantasy land, ensuring that we don’t fall into the ‘<a href="https://rippedbody.com/dreambulkeritis/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">dream bulk’ trap</a>, gain a ton of fat, and waste a lot of time and effort dieting it off again.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1595914886263"><strong class="schema-faq-question">Is there a genetic limit to muscle growth.</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">Yes. As you advance as a trainee, you can expect diminishing returns to your training efforts. You’ll make the best gains during the first year of serious training, as long as you get your nutrition strategy right also. See my free guide: <a href="https://rippedbody.com/complete-diet-nutrition-set-up-guide/">The RippedBody Nutrition Setup Guide</a>.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1595915030751"><strong class="schema-faq-question">What is FFMI?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">FFMI (Fat-Free Mass Index) measures the amount of muscle mass someone has relative to their height. In other words, it’s a way to quantify how jacked people are.<br><br>It is calculated as follows: <em>FFMI = fat-free mass in kg ÷ (height in meters)²</em><br><br>You can use FFMI to estimate how close you are to your genetic potential, which can help inform your training decisions.</p></div><div class="schema-faq-section" id="faq-question-1595917042925"><strong class="schema-faq-question">Why do muscles stop growing?</strong><p class="schema-faq-answer">It’s not known for sure, but it may be a surface area to muscle volume ratio problem related to energy and oxygen delivery.<br><br>Once muscle fibers get too big, it may be too energetically costly to build more protein and maintain the fiber as it is, and there may be something telling the fiber that it’s no longer economical to build. <br><br>For example, when muscle fibers get too large, they can start having a problem with inorganic phosphate accumulation which can affect contractile function. Additionally, mitochondrial density tends to drop, capillary density per fiber tends to be maintained, but per unit of cross-sectional area tends to decrease. (Source: <a href="https://the-stronger-by-science-podcast.simplecast.com/episodes/reading-research-the-placebo-diet-muscle-memory-and-alex-kolliari-turner?t=2h29m52s" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Stronger by Science Episode</a> <a href="https://the-stronger-by-science-podcast.simplecast.com/episodes/reading-research-the-placebo-diet-muscle-memory-and-alex-kolliari-turner?t=2h29m52s" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">20: Reading Research, the Placebo Diet, Muscle Memory, and Alex Kolliari-Turner</a>)</p></div></div></div><form action="https://app.kit.com/forms/7749683/subscriptions" style="background-color:#fffefa;border-radius:10px" class="seva-form formkit-form" method="post" data-sv-form="7749683" data-uid="8462769b72" data-format="inline" data-version="5" min-width="400 500 600 700"><div data-style="full" style="--bg-border-radius:5px"><div data-element="column" class="formkit-column"><div class="formkit-header" style="color:#333333;font-weight:700" data-element="header"><h2><strong>STOP GUESSING. For the majority of my 20s, I fell for the scams of the fitness industry. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="🤦🏻‍♂️" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f926-1f3fb-200d-2642-fe0f.svg">
⠀
This site is my revenge. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="😬" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f62c.svg">
⠀
Since 2011, I’ve written guides explaining how we get results to help readers avoid the same path of frustration. We’ve helped over 2,000 busy working guys transform their bodies WITHOUT supplements, fad diets, or the process dominating their lives.

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>I find this interesting, I scored 28, I’m 1.85m tall and 120kg at 19.7% bf (measured last night with digital calipers). I know I am a genetic freak but I only started lifting again 9-10months ago. I’ve been on and off over the years but I’d argue my genetics are probably better than even Arnold’s. I have 32 inch quads, a 55 inch chest and 19 inch arms completely natural after 10 months. 12 months ago I weighed 25 kilos less. My transformation pics are on Instagram under @adam_yates_physique if it interests any of you. I’m planning on taking this as far as I can and defo got my eye on the prize with pushing my genetic limits. I want to enter the IFBB completely natural and prove everyone wrong that steroids are not the be all and end all of bodybuilding!</p>

    Joe Franklin
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-date" title="August 13, 2018 12:09">
    <i class="far fa-clock" aria-hidden="true"></i>

</div>
    
    
    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/maximum-muscular-potential/#comment-66810"></i></span>
</div>
</div>
    
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>I think the FFMI is really off base.  25 years ago I had an FFMI of 28 completely drug free.  My main supplement was peanut butter.  I was strong and fairly muscular, but really nothing special.  By no means was I gifted. I had friends, who I would estimate would score higher than I did.  If you check the scores, the FFMI really goes higher the more you weigh.  SO, you can have a really high FFMI with a fairly high fat percentage.  For instance someone who is 5 feet 9 inches and weighed 245 with 20% body fat would have a high score.</p>

</div>

    </a>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>If it was really off base, then it’s possible you are in a population pocket with a very good gene pool, or just underestimated your body fat percentage. The latter is more likely due to the limitations of the tools we have available to assess it. I’ve written on this <a href="https://rippedbody.com/how-calculate-body-fat-percentage/">here</a>, <a href="https://rippedbody.com/is-it-better-to-forget-about-body-fat-percentage/">here</a> and even invited one of the world’s best stats geeks on my podcast to talk about the subject (listen <a href="https://rippedbody.com/podcast-james-krieger/">here</a>).</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-date" title="August 5, 2016 08:33">
    <i class="far fa-clock" aria-hidden="true"></i>
    August 5, 2016 08:33
</div>
    
    
    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/maximum-muscular-potential/#comment-45538"></i></span>
</div>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-reply-to">
    <i class="far fa-comments"></i>
    Reply to 
    <a href="#comment-45531">
        Matthias Flehl
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Matthias.</p>
<p>I recall the one (but can’t find the link), and I believed it showed that adaptations to training (muscle growth) get less as we age. The real question is whether this matters at all, and I’d say no. You’re not getting any younger, so it’s pointless worrying that you’re not getting the gains you would have. Higher lean body mass levels are linked with longevity and quality of life in old age (being able to sit and then stand from the toilet by yourself for longer) and studies show time and time again the positive effects of strength training in the elderly.</p>
<p>Let’s do a little more mental masturbation here. At 15 we will take advantage of the growth spurt, so let’s set that aside for a moment and consider 20-60 instead. The difference we’re talking about is a <em>gradual</em> reduction in recovery capacity, more injury considerations, and an increase in the lower threshold of protein intake in one sitting for MPS to take place (~20g – ~35g, meaning fewer meals with more protein per sitting are better, or just keeping protein intake high a good idea). The latter is irrelevant outside of extremely high meal frequencies or low protein intake, the injury part we have no control over (what’s done is done), so we get to the first item – recovery capacity.</p>
<p>Recovery capacity and adaptions to training will be different in different people. Some blessed, some not, most of us average. Forget that. Will YOU get the same training effect, all things being equal at 50 as you would 20? Not quite.</p>
<p>However, all things aren’t equal. You probably sleep better now, have access to better information, training modalities, money (should you need to see a trainer to guide you around any injuries) and your quality of diet is probably higher. You probably have a lot more commitments which make hitting the gym harder too… Well, I guess it’s swings and roundabouts.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-date" title="January 18, 2016 13:42">

    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/maximum-muscular-potential/#comment-43093"></i></span>
</div>
</div>
    
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Andy,<br>
Many of the pages I found for adjusted FFMI added a coefficient for height; ie adjusted FFMI = FFMI + 6 x (height – 1.8).</p>
<p>I just had a read of the pubmed extract, and the ratio is REVERSED! <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="🙂" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f642.svg"></p>
<p>That is “We then added a slight correction of 6.3 x (1.80 m – height) to normalize these values to the height of a 1.8-m man”.</p>
<p>So the formulae for adjusted FFMI = LBM/(Height^2) + 6.3 x (1.8-Height).</p>
<p>In my case, LBM = 92kg and Height = 1.91m<br>
therefore adjusted FFMI = 92/1.91^2 + 6.3 x (1.8-1.91)<br>
= 24.52</p>
<p>much more reasonable than the suggested 26 which occurred when the coefficient ended up with a positive value adjustment.</p>
<p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="🙂" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f642.svg"></p>
<p>Kind Regards,</p>
<p>Trent</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Trent, in the study text it’s actually 6.1, where the abstract 6.3 (likely a typo). Every online calculator I’ve found appears to get this wrong. Based on how I’m reading the study, the taller people had a higher FFMI, even with less muscle development, so their adjusted formula reduces the number for tall people and increases it for short people. They explain this by pointing out that taller people are usually also wider/thicker, and the standard FFMI formula is 2-dimensional, not 3-dimensional. I’d love to know where all this went wrong. Since most online calculators seem to use the same source code, one mistake seems to have been copied quite a number of times.</p>

</div>

</div>
</div>
    
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Thanks Andy,<br>
I note though, the adjusted FFMI suggests adding to the index for heights above 1.8m.. ie adjusted is FFMI + 6*(height-1.8).. In my case, this takes my current 25 up to 26…</p>
<p>I’ll have a DEXA done in a couple of weeks to make sure my numbers on BF% & LBM are correct… but i’m unlikely to have bf >12%, being i’m only 13 weeks post comp (INBA physique) and haven’t been binging or on a massive surplus…. </p>
<p>Has anyone ever calculated a formulae for muscle mass index, ie maximum genetic potential of muscle mass based on height, size, bone density etc?</p>
<p>Perhaps this will be a good question for Eric Helms (he’s co-presenting camps with Layne Norton in Sydney this April, I’m lucky enough to have gotten a place to attend)..</p>
<p>Thanks again.</p>
<p>Trent.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>HI Andy,<br>
Nice article and a good read in the comments too – it’s great to see your engagement with your readers and fans!</p>
<p>Anyway, on the FFMI; is there a way to “adjust for height” for tall blokes? </p>
<p>I’m 191cm, and run a LBM of 92kg at the moment; that’s a FFMI of 25.2..<br>
100% natty, 35 years old and training for only 3 years. </p>
<p>I wouldn’t call myself genetically gifted, growing up as a very skinny, lanky kid & teen.. Whilst my 3 years of lifting has seen me reach half-decent lift 1RM results (Sq 185kg, Dead 245kg, Bench 120kg)..</p>
<p>I still have a good number of years ahead of me and still have room to grow. </p>
<p>I think part of the problem with the formulae is no coefficient for bone density/mass – especially as a taller person further builds on this through compound lifting..</p>
<p>Keep up the great work – btw your page helped me with my first introduction to training back 3 years ago.. Links from Fitocracy brought me to your site, and I used your training recommendations in hand with IF to hit sub 9% for the first time in 2013..</p>

</div>

    <div id="comment-41519" class="wpd-comment-right">

    <div class="wpd-comment-date" title="September 5, 2015 09:39">

    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/maximum-muscular-potential/#comment-41519"></i></span>
</div>
</div>
    
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>I don’t believe the guy in the example is or could be natural.<br>
All the point  made in this article, as well as in the Eric Helms one, is based in the assumption that the FFMI of the Mr Americas is remotley acurate which i don’t think is the case.<br>
Look at the FFMI of John Grimek: it goes from 24 to 26.9 in the timespam of one year. Assuming he is 1,75 tall, he would have gained 9kg (20lbs) of lean mass in that time. 9kg of muscle in one year is a great acomplishment for a begginer, it’s just impossible to put that much in one year (or at all) on top of an already Mr America winning physique.<br>
Also, if you look at photos of these guys, for example Steeve Reeves and Jack Delinger side by side (<a href="https://2.bp.blogspot.com/-0ADfy0vRAuI/TpE5sTE9TpI/AAAAAAAADuY/vss7UIDche4/s1600/one.jpg" rel="nofollow ugc">https://2.bp.blogspot.com/-0ADfy0vRAuI/TpE5sTE9TpI/AAAAAAAADuY/vss7UIDche4/s1600/one.jpg</a>), they look they have the same degree of muscularity. There’s no way one FFMI is 23 and the other 28.<br>
These FFMI of former Mr Americas are completely urreliable to draw any valid conclusion.<br>
A  FFMI of 25 is actually great in real life (outside the internet). Its someone with very good genetics who trained hard for years, someone that look big to most people and will be acused of juicing frequently. Most wont reach a FFMI of 25 naturally. There should be some freaks capable of reaching 26, but more than that? I don’t buy into it, specially considering how financially and professionay rewarding is to  be a fake natural bodybuilder, trainer  or youtuber nowadays.</p>
<p>I agree with Casey Butt when he says : “The truth is 20 pounds of real, permanent muscle would transform your body. Most magazines and websites make it seem like 20 pounds of muscle is nothing …like your grandmother could gain that much. The reality is, if you gain 20 pounds of muscle this year everyone will notice and they’ll probably whisper behind your back that you’re on steroids – my friends did and I didn’t gain nearly that much in any one year. Gain 30 pounds of muscle (above your normal, healthy adult weight) and you’ll be carrying as much muscle as a world-class drug-free bodybuilder.”</p>

</div>

</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-date" title="September 5, 2015 12:33">
    <i class="far fa-clock" aria-hidden="true"></i>
    September 5, 2015 12:33
</div>
    
    
    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/maximum-muscular-potential/#comment-41521"></i></span>

    <div class="wpd-reply-to">
    <i class="far fa-comments"></i>
    Reply to 
    <a href="#comment-41519">
        Victor
    </a>
</div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Victor, thanks for commenting. I’ll reply in order.</p>
<p><strong>1. Re: Grimek.</strong> He made a jump from 24 to 26.9 FFMI in a single year, 1940-1941 – the years when we’re fairly sure steroids weren’t available. Essentially the question is, “What possible explanations are there for the 2.9 jump in FFMI (~20.7lbs in stage weight difference) in a single year, other than steroid use?”</p>
<p>Well, back then people were less consistent with individual stage condition (how shredded they were), and the levels of conditioning expected were not what they are now. A less lean, more hydrated and glycogen filled Grimek on stage, with a year of solid training behind him, could easily explain the 20lb increase, and perhaps overall size was more important than being leaner back then, to a point.</p>
<p>2. An interesting point. I have assumed that the FFMI figures are correct, for lack of choice really. I would’t try and read too much into a single photo, with different angles.</p>
<p>3. <em>“There should be some freaks capable of reaching 26, but more than that? I don’t buy into it.”</em> – Fair enough, I disagree though. (The math of why I think it’s possible is covered in the article.) There are more people in the sport now, so the chances of one of the few people in the world with potential to reach 26+ starting it are higher. And though the financial rewards of becoming a fake natty are going to overpower a lot of people’s morals, some people won’t do it simply out of principle, knowing they have to look at themselves in the mirror each day.</p>
<p>– A man walks out of the coffee shop leaving his briefcase behind. You look to see if you can find a name on it, and it pops open, revealing $10mil in cash inside. Now, a lot of people are going to silently take the money and disappear. – Not everyone though.</p>
<p>4. I absolutely agree with your last paragraph.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-space"></div>
    <div class="wpd-comment-link wpd-hidden">
    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/maximum-muscular-potential/#comment-41524"></i></span>

    <div class="wpd-reply-to">
    <i class="far fa-comments"></i>

        Andy Morgan
    </a>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Andy, while i don’t agree completely i can see tour points.</p>
<p>You are right about the variability of the conditioning these guys presented at contest day. The carb intake and short use of very high volume in the weeks prior to competition sure could have an effect on lean mass. But 20lbs would be more what a fighter varies in lean mass  with weight-in dehydratation. Still possible, just not likely in my opinion.<br>
It’s just that one would expect that the top genetic gifted individuals, with no access to drugs, training with the same goal, would end up somewhat in the same ballpark of muscle mass. But the values vary so much, even in the same individual, that makes me very suspicious of the acuraccy of the measurements. Also because i don’t see that crazy variation in old photos or videos. But i could be wrong.</p>
<p>Let just forget the number aspect for a moment. The old school guys had a very different look, this is not only a matter of body fat percentage, you didn’t see the “full muscle”, “thin skin” pumped look. Look at Clancy Ross: <a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_VCdnLTHSi8" rel="nofollow ugc">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_VCdnLTHSi8</a>. Acording to the chart, his FFMI is over 26, even then doesnt have “that look”.</p>
<p>I agree that there are many individuals that refuse to pull the fake natty routine due to their morals. Unfortunetly these guys are often the guys who have their advice discredited as trainers because they are being compared to drug assisted standards of muscle mass, or are the guys losing a natural bodybuilding competition because they are competing against users.</p>
<p>Just as a closing thought: I live in Brazil, fitness is a big thing  here , lots of gyms, so a very large talent pool. But at the same time, while selling steroids is a crime here, the possession is not, and its not as much seem as a morally reproved thing as in some other countries, and even some supplement sponsored competitors admit using.  So here people talk more openly about it, and people are more realistic about what can and can’t be achived naturally. Its interesting that here you almost don’t see these huge naturals genetic freaks that appear so frequently in the US.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Andy, thanks for the ideas.  I am going to be starting my bulk on August 1, so I will be taking in more food for sure, and will change up my workout to see if I can find something that will work better during this bulk.  Since I was on my cut for summer, I have just focused on going heavy, but I think I kept volume to high when I should not have.  I guess for me, it just takes about 1.5 – 2 hours of lifting before I start feeling fatigued, or like I am hitting a wall…..</p>

</div>

    <span wpd-tooltip="Comment Link" wpd-tooltip-position="left"><i class="fas fa-link" aria-hidden="true" data-wpd-clipboard="https://rippedbody.com/maximum-muscular-potential/#comment-40941"></i></span>
</div>
</div>
    
    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hey Andy,<br>
So Kim you are saying is natural, even w/ the amt. of definition he has?  I mean those quads are insane.</p>
<p>I guess for me, I am just getting to the point where I am disheartened/frustrated.<br>
I am 6’1″ (lanky, very small wrists and ankles), been lifting since I was 17 yrs old, I am 37 now.<br>
I did a lot of basketball, volleyball and stuff in my 20’s and late teens.<br>
And since 2013, I have started IF as well as really giving my workout sessions 100% energy and focus.<br>
But it just seems like I will never get to ~180 lbs, w/ <8% BF<br>
And my high end weight lifting seems to be limited by my joints at this point.  Once I start going heavy, then I start feeling it in my joints.</p>
<p>Looking at some posts by Gregory, perhaps I need to back off my workouts some…<br>
He seems to do 3x/week.</p>
<p>Worth a try I guess.</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Seth, thanks for the comment and questions.<br>
<em>“So Kim you are saying is natural…?”</em><br>
– Nope, I’m saying it is within the realm of possibility that he is.<br>
<em>Looking at some posts by Gregory, perhaps I need to back off my workouts some…<br>
He seems to do 3x/week.</em><br>
– It’s good to look at what some else is doing and draw inspiration from it, but you want to get to a point where you know how to find tune your own training. Joints ache? Options: Eat more / reduce training load (use lighter weights, higher rep sets, but maintain volume) / reduce volume or frequency. Change one variable at a time, so you can effectively gauge efficacy, then fine tune from there.</p>

</div>

  var bodyClassList = document.body.classList;
  var optinMonsterSegment = 'generic'; // Default

  // Slug arrays
  var trainingSegmentSlugs = ['training']; // Add more training slugs as needed
  var nutritionSegmentSlugs = ['nutrition']; // Add more nutrition slugs as needed

  // Function to check if any slug exists in body class
  function segmentSlugExists(slugArray) {
    return slugArray.some(function(slug) {
      return bodyClassList.contains('category-' + slug);
    });
  }

  // Check for training or nutrition segments
  if (segmentSlugExists(trainingSegmentSlugs)) {
    optinMonsterSegment = 'training';
  } else if (segmentSlugExists(nutritionSegmentSlugs)) {
    optinMonsterSegment = 'nutrition';
  }

metadata.article:author: https://www.facebook.com/rippedbodycom
metadata.author: Andy Morgan
metadata.description: There are limitations to the maximum drug-free muscular potential models out there and you need to be aware of them. This article will discuss them in depth
metadata.og:description: 1. There are limitations to the maximum drug-free muscular potential models out there. 2. It's useful to be aware of them, but you shouldn't necessarily cap yourself at what these calculators would peg you as being capable of achieving. 3. As fun as it would be, it is not possible to simply make a calculation to determine whether someone is 'roided to the gills or not.
metadata.og:title: Maximum Genetic Muscular Potential | RippedBody.com

data.title: Maximum Muscle Potential — What is your genetic muscular potential?
